# SECTION 21 — ONCOLOGY: LIQUID BIOPSY
## Translation Key: Laboratory Tests Mapped to Body Codx Operations

---

## Galleri (Multi-Cancer Early Detection)

**Body Codx Operation:** Cross-Operation (Defense primary, with Regulation, Transduction, and Elimination co-involvement)
**What It Measures:** Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) methylation patterns in peripheral blood to detect a cancer signal across 50+ cancer types, with predicted cancer signal origin (CSO) — tissue-of-origin localization.
**Sample Type:** Peripheral blood (two 10 mL Streck Cell-Free DNA BCT tubes)
**Fasting Required:** No
**Ordering Requirement:** Physician order required. Not a replacement for standard-of-care screenings (mammography, colonoscopy, cervical screening, lung CT).

### What It Measures

The Galleri test, developed by GRAIL (now a subsidiary of Illumina), is a multi-cancer early detection (MCED) test that interrogates cell-free DNA methylation patterns in peripheral blood to identify a shared cancer signal across more than 50 cancer types — including many cancers that have no existing screening test (pancreatic, ovarian, liver, esophageal, head and neck, lymphoma, and others). [FACT] The test does not look for specific mutations. Instead, it reads the methylation landscape of circulating cfDNA fragments — the methyl-group patterns that determine which genes are silenced and which are active. Cancer cells produce aberrant methylation patterns that differ systematically from normal tissue, and when these cells undergo apoptosis or necrosis, their methylated cfDNA enters the bloodstream. [FACT]

The core technology is targeted methylation sequencing: Galleri uses bisulfite conversion followed by next-generation sequencing across more than 100,000 methylation regions spanning approximately 1 million CpG sites. A proprietary machine learning classifier trained on the Circulating Cell-free Genome Atlas (CCGA) study cohort of over 15,000 participants then distinguishes cancer-derived methylation patterns from the background noise of normal cfDNA (which originates primarily from hematopoietic cell turnover). Critically, the classifier also predicts the cancer signal origin (CSO) — the tissue from which the cancer signal most likely originates — using tissue-specific methylation signatures. This is possible because methylation patterns are the molecular "address labels" of cell identity: a liver cell, a pancreatic cell, and a lung cell all carry the same DNA sequence, but their methylation patterns are profoundly different. When cancer-derived cfDNA appears in the blood, the methylation pattern reveals not just "cancer is present" but "cancer in this tissue type." [FACT]

### Ranges

| Parameter | Result Category | Clinical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Cancer Signal | **Not Detected** | No cancer signal identified; does not rule out cancer. Resume standard screening. |
| Cancer Signal | **Detected** | Cancer signal identified. Requires diagnostic confirmation (imaging, biopsy). |
| Cancer Signal Origin (CSO) | **Single tissue prediction** | Highest-confidence tissue of origin (e.g., "Hepatobiliary," "Upper GI," "Lung") — directs diagnostic workup. |
| Cancer Signal Origin (CSO) | **Two tissue predictions** | Two possible tissue origins listed by probability — broader workup indicated. |
| False Positive Rate | **~0.5%** | Specificity >99.5% in the CCGA study (Liu et al., 2020; PMID: 32546030 [NEEDS VERIFICATION]). |
| Sensitivity (all stages) | **~51.5%** (stages I–III varies by cancer type) | Overall sensitivity across 50+ cancers. Stage I sensitivity ~16.8%, Stage II ~40.4%, Stage III ~77%, Stage IV >90%. Sensitivity is highest for cancers with high cfDNA shedding rates. [FACT] |
| Sensitivity (12 pre-specified high-mortality cancers) | **~67.6%** (stages I–III) | Higher sensitivity in the cancers that account for ~63% of U.S. cancer deaths. [FACT] |

**Functional Optimal:** There is no "optimal range" for Galleri in the way a metabolic marker has one. The functional optimal is a "Not Detected" result combined with continued standard-of-care screening. A "Detected" result at any time requires urgent diagnostic workup. [INTERPRETATION]

### What Deviation Signals

**Cancer Signal Detected:** A positive Galleri result means the classifier identified methylation patterns in cfDNA that are consistent with a cancer signal. This is not a diagnosis — it is a signal requiring confirmation. The positive predictive value (PPV) depends on the prevalence of cancer in the tested population; in an asymptomatic screening population, a positive result has an approximate PPV of 38–44% across all cancer types, meaning roughly 4 in 10 positive results will be confirmed cancers on diagnostic workup. [FACT — from PATHFINDER study, Beer et al., Lancet 2023; NEEDS VERIFICATION on exact PPV figures] The remaining results represent false positives, which may include clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) — age-related somatic mutations in blood stem cells that produce aberrant cfDNA but are not cancer. GRAIL's methylation-based approach was designed specifically to minimize CHIP-driven false positives (a major limitation of mutation-based liquid biopsies), and CHIP interference is substantially lower than in ctDNA mutation-based assays. [FACT]

**Cancer Signal Not Detected:** A negative result does not exclude cancer. The overall sensitivity of ~51.5% means that approximately half of cancers will not produce a detectable signal, particularly early-stage cancers with low tumor burden and low cfDNA shedding rates. Cancers with low shedding rates (brain tumors, renal cell carcinoma, thyroid cancers, most stage I solid tumors) are less likely to produce a detectable signal. Hematologic malignancies (lymphomas, myelomas) and cancers with high shedding (hepatobiliary, pancreatic, ovarian, lung) are more reliably detected. [FACT] This test is designed as a complement to — not replacement for — existing screening tests. Galleri fills the "screening gap" for the 70%+ of cancer deaths that occur from cancers for which no screening test currently exists. [INTERPRETATION]

**CSO Accuracy:** When a cancer signal is detected and confirmed, the cancer signal origin prediction is accurate in approximately 88–93% of cases (predicting the correct tissue type within the first or second CSO prediction). [FACT — CCGA sub-study data; NEEDS VERIFICATION on exact percentage] This directs diagnostic workup efficiently — rather than a full-body scan, clinicians can target the indicated tissue. When CSO accuracy fails, it is most commonly in cancers with overlapping methylation profiles (e.g., upper GI vs. hepatobiliary) or in metastatic cancers where the metastatic site sheds more cfDNA than the primary. [INTERPRETATION]

### Pattern Recognition

- **Galleri + elevated CA-125 or CA 19-9 in an asymptomatic patient:** When Galleri detects a cancer signal with CSO prediction of ovarian or pancreatic origin AND a tumor marker (CA-125, CA 19-9) is independently elevated, the convergence dramatically increases the pre-test probability of cancer. This is the multi-modal early detection pattern — no single test is diagnostic, but convergence warrants urgent workup. [INTERPRETATION]

- **Galleri "Not Detected" + persistent constitutional symptoms (weight loss, night sweats, new fatigue):** The absence of a Galleri signal does not override clinical suspicion. Cancers with low cfDNA shedding rates (brain, renal, thyroid, many stage I cancers) are below the test's detection threshold. Clinical suspicion drives workup, not a negative screening result. This is the false-reassurance trap — a negative MCED result is not clearance. [FACT]

- **Galleri "Detected" with CSO pointing to a cancer type already covered by standard screening (e.g., breast, colorectal, lung):** In these cases, Galleri has detected what standard screening may have already found or will find — but the Galleri signal may precede imaging detection by months to years, particularly for cancers that begin shedding cfDNA before reaching radiographically detectable size. The question becomes whether Galleri is detecting earlier or confirming simultaneously. [HYPOTHESIS]

- **Serial Galleri testing (annual) with signal conversion from "Not Detected" to "Detected":** Year-over-year signal conversion is the strongest liquid biopsy finding — it represents a newly detectable cancer signal in a previously negative individual. This is the screening interval detection event: a tumor has grown large enough or begun shedding cfDNA at a sufficient rate to cross the detection threshold. Urgent workup is indicated. [INTERPRETATION]

### Intervention Levers

- **Standard-of-care screening continuation:** Galleri is additive, never substitutive. Mammography, colonoscopy, low-dose CT for high-risk smokers, Pap/HPV screening — all continue regardless of Galleri result. The cancers these tests detect (breast, colorectal, cervical, lung) are the cancers with the strongest screening-to-survival evidence. [FACT]

- **Diagnostic workup protocol for positive results:** A "Detected" result with CSO prediction should trigger directed imaging (PET-CT is the most common first step, with targeted MRI or CT of the indicated tissue). Biopsy of any identified lesion confirms or refutes the Galleri signal. The PATHFINDER study demonstrated that the median time from Galleri "Detected" to diagnostic resolution was approximately 57 days. [NEEDS VERIFICATION] Oncology referral is standard for any confirmed cancer. [FACT]

- **CHIP evaluation:** For positive Galleri results that do not confirm cancer on workup, clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) should be evaluated — particularly in patients >65 years, as CHIP prevalence increases with age (present in ~10% of individuals >65 and >20% of individuals >70). A dedicated CHIP panel (VAF of DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1, TP53, JAK2) can clarify. [FACT]

- **Annual re-screening cadence:** GRAIL recommends annual testing for the target population (adults ≥50 years, or those at elevated cancer risk). The clinical utility depends on catching cancers during the "window of interception" — the period between molecular detectability and symptomatic presentation. [INTERPRETATION]

- **Lifestyle and chemoprevention integration:** A negative Galleri result in a high-risk individual (family history, BRCA carrier, Lynch syndrome) is not reassurance — it is motivation to maintain aggressive prevention. Exercise (≥150 min/week moderate or ≥75 min vigorous), anti-inflammatory diet, weight management, alcohol restriction, and disease-specific chemoprevention (e.g., aspirin for Lynch-associated CRC, tamoxifen/aromatase inhibitors for BRCA-related breast cancer) remain the upstream levers. Galleri catches what prevention missed. [INTERPRETATION]

### Body Codx Three-Density Reading

**Body:** Galleri reads the Defense operation at its most fundamental surveillance layer — the capacity of the organism to detect cells that have broken the coherence contract. Every cell in the body carries the same genome, but cancer is the cell that has rewritten its own epigenetic program to escape the rules of tissue identity. The methylation patterns Galleri reads are the molecular signatures of this coherence breach. At body density, this test asks: has any tissue in the system begun operating outside its design parameters? The answer is binary — signal detected or not — but the implications cascade across every operation. A detected cancer signal means Synthesis has been co-opted (tumor growth), Defense has been evaded (immune escape), Regulation has failed (checkpoint failure), and Elimination is being overwhelmed (apoptosis resistance). This is not a single-operation event. It is a cross-operation decoherence. [INTERPRETATION]

**Soul:** At soul density, Galleri represents the capacity for honest self-inventory — the willingness to look for what you don't want to find. Most people avoid comprehensive screening because the prospect of a positive result is more threatening than the disease itself. The methylation signature is the body's version of a subconscious pattern that has been running long enough to leave a trace — something that started as a single cell's deviation and has grown into a detectable signal. The soul-density question is not "do I have cancer" but "am I willing to know what is growing in me that I haven't been willing to see?" The courage to screen is itself a soul-level act of self-fidelity — choosing truth over comfort. The CSO prediction adds another dimension: not just "something is wrong" but "here is where it lives." This is the diagnostic precision of honest self-examination. [INTERPRETATION]

**Spirit:** At spirit density, the multi-cancer early detection pattern mirrors consciousness's capacity to scan its own arising content for the signature of contraction — the way awareness learns to recognize the fragmentation pattern of a self-referential loop before it fully crystallizes into a fixed identity structure. Just as cfDNA circulates freely and carries the mutational trace of cells that have deviated from organismic coherence, phenomenal content circulates through awareness carrying the "mutational signature" of where consciousness has begun to mistake a local structure for its ground. Early detection here is the difference between inquiry and entrenchment — catching the cancer of identification before it metastasizes into a whole personality structure organized around a lie. The tissue-of-origin prediction maps to spiritual discernment: not just "something is off" but "this is the domain where consciousness has contracted." [INTERPRETATION]

### Clinical Pearl

Galleri is the first commercially available test that attempts to answer the question that has haunted oncology for decades: what about the cancers we can't screen for? The test's greatest clinical contribution is not in the cancers already screened (breast, colorectal, cervical, lung) but in the ~70% of cancer types for which no screening test previously existed. In the PATHFINDER study, approximately 73% of cancers detected by Galleri were cancer types not targeted by any existing guideline-recommended screening test. [NEEDS VERIFICATION] The limitation is sensitivity at early stages — the test is best at detecting cancers that are already shedding meaningful cfDNA, which often means later-stage or high-shedding tumor types. The methylation-based approach was a deliberate design choice to reduce false positives from CHIP (a problem that plagued mutation-based cfDNA assays), and the >99.5% specificity is clinically meaningful in an asymptomatic screening population where false positives generate anxiety, unnecessary procedures, and healthcare costs. The CSO prediction is what makes the test actionable — without tissue-of-origin guidance, a positive multi-cancer screen would require an exhaustive and expensive full-body workup. Galleri converts "you might have cancer somewhere" into "look here first." [FACT with INTERPRETATION]

---

## Guardant360

**Body Codx Operation:** Defense
**What It Measures:** Comprehensive liquid biopsy analyzing circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) from a standard blood draw. 74-gene panel detecting single nucleotide variants (SNVs), insertions/deletions (indels), copy number amplifications (CNAs), and gene fusions — plus microsatellite instability (MSI) and tumor mutational burden (TMB). Used for therapy selection in advanced solid tumors.
**Sample Type:** Peripheral blood (two 10 mL Streck or Guardant blood collection tubes)
**Fasting Required:** No
**Ordering Requirement:** Physician order required. FDA-approved for NSCLC (multiple companion diagnostic indications). Clinical utility established for multiple advanced solid tumors.

### What It Measures

Guardant360 is a comprehensive genomic profiling liquid biopsy developed by Guardant Health that analyzes circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) shed by tumors into the bloodstream. Unlike Galleri, which screens asymptomatic individuals for the presence of any cancer, Guardant360 is designed for patients with known or suspected advanced solid tumors who need actionable genomic information to guide therapy. It is a treatment-selection tool, not a screening tool. [FACT]

The technology platform isolates cfDNA from a standard blood draw, enriches tumor-derived fragments using proprietary digital sequencing technology, and performs ultra-deep sequencing (>10,000x typical coverage) on a curated panel of 74 clinically relevant cancer genes. The ultra-deep coverage is essential because ctDNA may represent as little as 0.1–0.5% of total cfDNA in the blood — the "needle in a haystack" problem documented across liquid biopsy literature (Diehn et al., 2014 [NEEDS VERIFICATION on specific citation]). At these depths, Guardant360 can detect variant allele frequencies (VAFs) as low as 0.04–0.1% for SNVs, enabling identification of actionable mutations even at very low tumor fractions. [FACT] The panel detects four categories of genomic alterations: (1) single nucleotide variants (SNVs) and indels across all 74 genes, (2) copy number amplifications (CNAs) in select genes (ERBB2/HER2, MET, EGFR, FGFR1/2, others), (3) gene fusions in select genes (ALK, RET, ROS1, NTRK1/2/3, FGFR2/3, others), and (4) biomarkers including microsatellite instability (MSI) status and blood-based tumor mutational burden (bTMB). [FACT]

### Ranges

| Parameter | Result Category | Clinical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Actionable Mutation Detected | e.g., **EGFR L858R**, **ALK fusion**, **KRAS G12C** | Matched targeted therapy available. Drug pairing listed on report (e.g., osimertinib for EGFR, sotorasib for KRAS G12C). |
| Resistance Mutation Detected | e.g., **EGFR T790M**, **ESR1 mutation** | Current therapy likely failing; mechanism of acquired resistance identified. Switch therapy per resistance profile. |
| MSI Status | **MSI-High (MSI-H)** | Eligible for pembrolizumab (Keytruda) regardless of tumor type (FDA pan-tumor approval, 2017). [FACT] |
| MSI Status | **MSI-Stable (MSS)** | Checkpoint immunotherapy less likely to be effective as monotherapy; standard chemotherapy or targeted therapy preferred. |
| Tumor Mutational Burden | **TMB-High (≥10 mut/Mb)** | Eligible for pembrolizumab (FDA pan-tumor approval, 2020). Higher neoantigen load predicts better immunotherapy response. [FACT] |
| Tumor Mutational Burden | **TMB-Low (<10 mut/Mb)** | Immunotherapy as monotherapy less likely to benefit; consider combination regimens or targeted approaches. |
| Variant Allele Frequency (VAF) | **Reported per variant** | Higher VAF = more abundant clone (likely driver). Low VAF variants may represent subclonal populations or CHIP (requires clinical correlation). |
| ctDNA Fraction | **Estimated or reported** | Very low ctDNA fraction (<0.5%) increases false negative risk and limits variant detection sensitivity. |

**Functional Optimal:** Not applicable in the conventional sense — this is a categorical/mutation-detection test. The "optimal" result is the identification of an actionable alteration that matches an FDA-approved targeted therapy, converting a cancer diagnosis from "chemotherapy by tumor type" to "precision therapy by molecular target." A result showing no actionable mutations in a patient with advanced cancer narrows the treatment options but is still diagnostically informative. [INTERPRETATION]

### What Deviation Signals

**Actionable Mutations Detected:** The primary clinical value of Guardant360 is identifying genomic alterations that match FDA-approved or NCCN guideline-recommended targeted therapies. In non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) — the best-studied indication — approximately 50–69% of patients harbor at least one guideline-recommended biomarker (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, NTRK, RET, MET, KRAS G12C, HER2). [FACT — NCCN NSCLC Guidelines; exact percentage NEEDS VERIFICATION] For these patients, a liquid biopsy identifying the alteration means the difference between empiric chemotherapy and precision-matched targeted therapy, which typically offers superior progression-free survival, response rates, and quality of life. Key examples: EGFR-mutant NSCLC treated with osimertinib (median PFS ~18.9 months vs. ~10.2 months for chemotherapy — FLAURA trial; NEEDS VERIFICATION); ALK-rearranged NSCLC treated with lorlatinib or alectinib; KRAS G12C mutant cancers treated with sotorasib or adagrasib. [FACT]

**Resistance Mutations:** When Guardant360 is ordered at disease progression, the detection of secondary resistance mutations (e.g., EGFR T790M after first-generation EGFR TKI failure, ESR1 mutations after aromatase inhibitor therapy in breast cancer, acquired ALK resistance mutations) reveals the mechanism by which the tumor escaped prior therapy. This is the molecular autopsy of treatment failure — and it is often actionable. Osimertinib was specifically developed for T790M-mediated resistance, and its identification via liquid biopsy is now the standard of care. [FACT] Guardant360's ability to detect resistance mutations from blood — without requiring a repeat tissue biopsy — is one of the test's most significant clinical advantages. Repeat tissue biopsies carry procedural risk (pneumothorax from lung biopsy, hemorrhage from liver biopsy) and may not be feasible in all patients. [FACT]

**MSI-H and TMB-H:** These are immunotherapy eligibility biomarkers. MSI-H status indicates mismatch repair deficiency — a failure in the DNA proofreading machinery that leads to hypermutation, particularly at microsatellite repeat sequences. MSI-H tumors accumulate thousands of mutations, generating neoantigens that make them visible to the immune system. This is why pembrolizumab received the first-ever tissue-agnostic (pan-tumor) FDA approval for MSI-H cancers in 2017 — the molecular biology transcends tumor type. [FACT] TMB-H (≥10 mutations/megabase) is a broader measure of mutational load and predicts immunotherapy response across tumor types (FDA pan-tumor approval for pembrolizumab + TMB-H, 2020). [FACT] Blood-based TMB (bTMB) measured by Guardant360 correlates with tissue-based TMB but is not perfectly concordant — bTMB may underestimate tissue TMB in tumors with low cfDNA shedding. [FACT]

### Pattern Recognition

- **Guardant360 detects EGFR T790M at progression on first-line

# SECTION 21 — ONCOLOGY: LIQUID BIOPSY
## Translation Key: Next-Generation Blood-Based Cancer Detection & Monitoring
### Mapped to Body Codx Operations

Liquid biopsy represents the frontier of cancer diagnostics — the ability to read cancer's molecular signature from a blood draw rather than a tissue biopsy. The foundational biology: all cells shed DNA fragments into the bloodstream as they die. Normal cells produce cell-free DNA (cfDNA) through orderly apoptosis — fragments of approximately 167 base pairs corresponding to nucleosome-wrapped DNA. Cancer cells also shed DNA, but their fragments carry tumor-specific mutations, copy number alterations, and aberrant methylation patterns. This circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) is a molecular telegram from the tumor, transmitted through the bloodstream, readable by the right instrument. [FACT]

The challenge is signal-to-noise. In early-stage cancer, ctDNA may constitute as little as 0.01–0.1% of total cfDNA — a whisper in a roaring river of normal cell turnover. [FACT] The platforms in this section represent distinct engineering solutions to this signal extraction problem: some use methylation patterns (Galleri, Grail Methylation Array), some use mutation panels with protein biomarkers (CancerSEEK), some use comprehensive genomic profiling for therapy selection (Guardant360, FoundationOne Liquid CDx), and some use tumor-informed personalized assays for recurrence monitoring (Signatera). Each occupies a different niche in the cancer detection-to-management arc.

From the Body Codx perspective, liquid biopsy is the diagnostic interface between Defense (immune surveillance, tumor recognition), Regulation (epigenetic control, methylation fidelity), and Elimination (clearance of damaged cells and DNA). When the machine is functioning, aberrant cells are eliminated before they accumulate enough mass to be detectable. When liquid biopsy detects a signal, it means the Elimination and Defense operations have already been partially overwhelmed — but critically, it means the system is still shedding evidence of the fight. The DNA in the blood is the body's attempt to tell you what is happening.

---

## Galleri (Multi-Cancer Early Detection)

**Body Codx Operation:** Cross-Operation (Defense / Regulation / Elimination)
**What It Measures:** Cell-free DNA methylation patterns across >100,000 methylation sites to detect cancer signals from 50+ cancer types, with tissue-of-origin prediction.
**Sample Type:** Blood (two 10 mL Streck cfDNA BCT tubes)
**Fasting Required:** No
**Test Developer:** GRAIL, Inc. (an Illumina company)

### What This Number Means

Galleri is the most ambitious multi-cancer early detection (MCED) test currently available. Rather than looking for mutations in specific genes — which vary enormously across cancer types — Galleri reads the methylation landscape of cell-free DNA circulating in the blood. [FACT] DNA methylation is the addition of methyl groups to cytosine residues at CpG dinucleotides, a fundamental epigenetic mechanism that controls gene expression. Every tissue type in the body has a characteristic methylation signature — liver cells methylate differently than lung cells, which methylate differently than colon cells. When cancer develops, the tumor cells acquire profoundly abnormal methylation patterns: global hypomethylation (loss of methylation across large genomic regions) paired with focal hypermethylation at tumor suppressor gene promoters. [FACT] These aberrant patterns are preserved on the cfDNA fragments shed by the tumor into the bloodstream, creating a cancer-specific methylation signal that is both detectable and tissue-specific.

The test uses targeted bisulfite sequencing to read methylation status at over 100,000 CpG sites across the genome, then applies a machine learning classifier trained on thousands of cancer and non-cancer samples to determine: (1) whether a cancer signal is detected, and (2) if detected, the predicted tissue of origin — which organ the cancer is most likely arising from. [FACT] This tissue-of-origin prediction is possible because the methylation patterns retain the fingerprint of the cell type that shed them, even after the DNA enters the bloodstream. The result is binary: "Cancer Signal Detected" or "Cancer Signal Not Detected," accompanied by a tissue-of-origin prediction if positive.

### Ranges

| Marker | Conventional Reference | Functional Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Cancer Signal Not Detected | Normal result | Does not rule out cancer — sensitivity varies by stage and type |
| Cancer Signal Detected | Abnormal — requires diagnostic workup | Positive predictive value ~43–44% in symptomatic populations [NEEDS VERIFICATION] |
| Tissue of Origin — Top 1 prediction | ~88% accuracy when cancer confirmed [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | Directs diagnostic workup to correct organ system |
| Tissue of Origin — Top 2 predictions | ~93% accuracy when cancer confirmed [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | Broadens search if top prediction is negative |
| Specificity (overall) | 99.5% across cancer types [FACT] | ~0.5% false positive rate — critical for screening populations |
| Sensitivity — Stage I | ~17–25% across cancer types [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | Low — not a replacement for standard screening |
| Sensitivity — Stage II | ~40–50% across cancer types [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | Moderate — improves with tumor burden |
| Sensitivity — Stage III | ~75–85% across cancer types [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | Good — most cancers detected at this stage |
| Sensitivity — Stage IV | ~90%+ across cancer types [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | High — extensive shedding from advanced disease |

### What Deviation Signals

**Cancer Signal Detected** indicates that the methylation classifier has identified a pattern consistent with a cancer-derived signal in the cfDNA. This does NOT confirm cancer — the positive predictive value depends heavily on the pre-test probability (prevalence in the tested population). In an asymptomatic screening population, even with 99.5% specificity, a positive result requires confirmatory diagnostic workup: imaging (PET-CT, MRI, or targeted organ imaging based on tissue-of-origin prediction), tissue biopsy, and potentially additional blood-based biomarkers. [INTERPRETATION] The tissue-of-origin prediction is the critical decision-support element — rather than a diagnostic wild goose chase across the entire body, the clinician can direct the workup to the predicted organ system.

**Cancer Signal Not Detected** provides meaningful but not absolute reassurance. The test's overall sensitivity is substantially higher for advanced-stage cancers (Stage III–IV) than for early-stage cancers (Stage I–II). [FACT] This means a negative result is more reliable for ruling out advanced disease than early disease. For the cancers that standard screening already covers well — breast (mammography), cervical (Pap/HPV), colorectal (colonoscopy), and lung (low-dose CT in high-risk populations) — Galleri is not a replacement. Its unique value proposition is in the 50+ cancer types that have NO standard screening test: pancreatic, ovarian, hepatobiliary, head and neck, lymphomas, and many others that are typically diagnosed at late stages. [INTERPRETATION]

The critical limitation is sensitivity at Stage I, where the tumor burden is small and ctDNA shedding is minimal. A Stage I pancreatic cancer may shed so little DNA that even the most sensitive methylation classifier cannot detect it. This is where the biology meets the engineering limit: the signal exists, but it is below the noise floor of current technology. Serial testing over time — annual Galleri testing — may partially address this by catching cancers as they progress from below-threshold to detectable, ideally while still localized. [HYPOTHESIS]

### Pattern Recognition

- **Galleri positive + tissue-of-origin concordant with imaging findings** = high-confidence cancer detection requiring tissue biopsy for histological confirmation and staging. The methylation pattern and imaging converge — proceed with standard oncologic workup directed at the predicted organ. [INTERPRETATION]
- **Galleri positive + tissue-of-origin discordant with initial imaging** = either (a) the imaging hasn't looked in the right place yet, (b) the tissue-of-origin prediction is in the ~12% that are incorrect on first prediction, or (c) the cancer is at a site difficult to image (e.g., peritoneal, retroperitoneal). Expand imaging to include the top 2 tissue-of-origin predictions. Consider PET-CT for whole-body survey. [INTERPRETATION]
- **Galleri negative in a high-risk individual** = reassuring but NOT definitive. If clinical suspicion remains high (family history, symptoms, other biomarker elevations), proceed with standard diagnostic workup regardless of Galleri result. No screening test overrides clinical judgment. [FACT]
- **Serial Galleri testing — negative to positive conversion** = a temporal signal of emerging cancer. This is potentially the most powerful use case: longitudinal monitoring where a shift from negative to positive represents a real biological change, not just a single cross-sectional measurement. The pre-test probability is substantially higher in this scenario. [HYPOTHESIS]

### Intervention Levers

- **Standard cancer screening compliance:** Galleri does NOT replace mammography, colonoscopy, cervical cancer screening, or lung cancer screening. It is additive — covering the cancers that standard screening misses. Patients must understand this clearly to prevent false security. [FACT]
- **Risk-stratified testing frequency:** Annual testing in individuals >50 years old, or earlier in those with significant cancer family history, prior cancer history, or cancer-predisposing germline mutations (BRCA1/2, Lynch syndrome, Li-Fraumeni, etc.). [INTERPRETATION]
- **Methylation-supportive interventions (upstream):** While Galleri reads the downstream consequence of epigenetic dysregulation, the upstream interventions that support methylation fidelity include adequate folate, B12, B6, betaine (TMG), and choline as methyl donors; avoidance of excessive alcohol (depletes folate, disrupts one-carbon metabolism); and maintenance of adequate vitamin D levels (modulates DNA methyltransferase activity). [INTERPRETATION]
- **Post-positive pathway:** Cancer Signal Detected → confirm tissue-of-origin → directed imaging → tissue biopsy → histopathological diagnosis → staging → multidisciplinary tumor board → treatment plan. Do not treat based on liquid biopsy alone. [FACT]
- **Psychosocial preparation:** Given the ~0.5% false positive rate, pre-test counseling should prepare the individual for the possibility of a positive result that does not indicate cancer. The anxiety of a positive MCED result pending confirmatory workup is substantial and should not be underestimated. [INTERPRETATION]

### Body Codx Three-Density Reading

**Body:** Galleri reads the methylation state of the cfDNA pool — the molecular exhaust of every cell in the body, filtered through the bloodstream. At body density, this is the Defense and Regulation operations in their most literal form: has the epigenetic regulatory machinery (Regulation) maintained methylation fidelity, or have cells escaped control and begun aberrant methylation programs? If aberrant methylation is detected, the Defense operation has failed to eliminate those cells before they accumulated enough mass to shed detectable signal. The test is reading the balance point between tumor growth rate (how fast the cancer is producing cfDNA) and clearance rate (how fast the liver and kidneys clear cfDNA, typically a half-life of 1–2 hours). [FACT] A positive result means production has exceeded the body's capacity to clear the evidence. The machine is sending a distress signal — literally, through the blood.

**Soul:** At soul density, Galleri operates at the level of Regulation as meaning-making: the capacity to distinguish what belongs from what has gone rogue. Methylation is the body's system of annotation — which genes should be expressed, which should remain silent, which chapters of the genetic code are relevant to this particular cell's identity. Cancer is a methylation crisis: cells that have lost the ability to read their own instructions, expressing what should be silent and silencing what should be expressed. The soul parallel is precise — when the annotation system that tells you who you are breaks down, you begin expressing patterns that don't belong to your design. The soul question Galleri raises is not "do I have cancer?" but "where in my system has the capacity for self-recognition failed?" [INTERPRETATION]

**Spirit:** At spirit density, the multi-cancer detection test maps to Awareness — the capacity to see what is actually present, without filtering, without denial. Many of the 50+ cancers Galleri screens for are cancers of the unseen: pancreatic (hidden behind the stomach), ovarian (deep in the pelvis), hepatobiliary (masked by liver function until late). These are the cancers that evade standard surveillance because they grow in darkness. The spirit reading is about what the system refuses to see — what has been growing in the shadows, below the threshold of awareness, until the molecular evidence becomes undeniable. Galleri is a tool for radical honesty with the body: are you willing to look at what is actually there, even when there was no reason to suspect it? [INTERPRETATION]

### Clinical Pearl

Galleri's greatest clinical value is not in what it finds but in what it finds *early enough to change the outcome*. The PATHFINDER study demonstrated that in a real-world screening population, Galleri detected cancer in 1.4% of tested individuals, with 38% of confirmed cancers found at Stage I or II. [NEEDS VERIFICATION] For cancers that are typically diagnosed at Stage III or IV (pancreatic, ovarian, hepatobiliary), even modest stage-shifting represents a meaningful survival benefit. The test is not perfect — it will miss many early-stage cancers and produce some false positives — but it occupies a diagnostic niche that previously did not exist: a single blood draw that screens for dozens of cancer types simultaneously. The key clinical discipline is integration, not replacement: Galleri alongside, not instead of, standard screening protocols. The person who gets a negative Galleri and skips their colonoscopy has misunderstood the tool entirely.

---

## Guardant360

**Body Codx Operation:** Defense
**What It Measures:** Comprehensive liquid biopsy analyzing circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) across a 74-gene panel for actionable genomic alterations, microsatellite instability (MSI), and tumor mutational burden (TMB) in patients with advanced solid tumors.
**Sample Type:** Blood (two 10 mL Streck cfDNA BCT tubes)
**Fasting Required:** No
**Test Developer:** Guardant Health
**FDA Status:** FDA-approved as companion diagnostic for specific targeted therapies

### What This Number Means

Guardant360 is a therapy-selection tool, not a screening tool. It is designed for patients who already have a diagnosed advanced solid tumor and need to identify actionable genomic targets to guide treatment decisions. [FACT] The test isolates cfDNA from a blood draw, then performs next-generation sequencing (NGS) across 74 cancer-relevant genes, detecting single nucleotide variants (SNVs), insertions/deletions (indels), copy number amplifications (CNAs), and gene fusions. Additionally, it reports microsatellite instability status (MSI-H vs. MSS) and tumor mutational burden (TMB-H defined as ≥16 mutations/megabase by blood-based calculation). [FACT]

The clinical logic is straightforward: many advanced cancers harbor specific genomic alterations that are targetable by approved or investigational therapies. EGFR mutations in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) predict response to osimertinib. ALK fusions predict response to alectinib or lorlatinib. BRAF V600E mutations predict response to encorafenib/binimetinib. PIK3CA mutations in breast cancer predict response to alpelisib. MSI-H status across solid tumors predicts response to pembrolizumab. TMB-H predicts response to immune checkpoint inhibitors broadly. [FACT] Guardant360 identifies these targets from a blood draw — avoiding the need for a repeat tissue biopsy, which in advanced disease may be technically difficult (inaccessible tumor location), risky (bleeding, pneumothorax), or yield insufficient tissue. The advantage of liquid biopsy in this context is that ctDNA represents a composite of all tumor deposits, including metastatic sites that may harbor different mutations than the primary tumor (tumor heterogeneity). [INTERPRETATION]

### Ranges

| Marker | Conventional Reference | Functional Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Actionable mutation detected | Directs targeted therapy selection | Cross-reference with NCCN guidelines for specific cancer type |
| No actionable mutation detected | Standard chemotherapy or immunotherapy | Does not mean tumor is mutation-free — ctDNA shedding may be insufficient |
| MSI-H (microsatellite instability — high) | Qualifies for pembrolizumab (pan-tumor) | Check germline Lynch syndrome testing if MSI-H found |
| MSS (microsatellite stable) | Standard therapy per cancer type | ~85% of solid tumors are MSS [FACT] |
| TMB-H (≥16 mut/Mb by blood) | Qualifies for pembrolizumab (pan-tumor) | Blood TMB correlates with but is not identical to tissue TMB [FACT] |
| TMB-L (<16 mut/Mb) | Standard therapy per cancer type | Does not preclude immunotherapy benefit in all contexts |
| Variant allele frequency (VAF) — high | Higher ctDNA shedding — larger tumor burden likely | VAF >5% generally indicates substantial tumor volume [INTERPRETATION] |
| VAF — low (<0.5%) | Lower tumor burden OR low-shedding tumor type | May require tissue confirmation for detected variants |
| No ctDNA detected | Possible low tumor burden, low-shedding tumor, or sampling timing | Does NOT mean disease-free — 10–15% of advanced cancers shed insufficient ctDNA for detection [NEEDS VERIFICATION] |

### What Deviation Signals

**Actionable mutation detected** is the primary clinical output. When Guardant360 identifies a genomic alteration with an FDA-approved or guideline-recommended targeted therapy, the therapeutic path changes fundamentally. A patient with advanced NSCLC and an EGFR L858R mutation has a dramatically different treatment algorithm (osimertinib, median PFS ~18 months) than a patient without this mutation (standard platinum doublet chemotherapy, median PFS ~5–6 months). [FACT] The variant allele frequency (VAF) reported alongside each mutation provides additional context — higher VAF generally indicates that a larger fraction of the cfDNA carries this mutation, suggesting it is a clonal (present in most tumor cells) rather than subclonal (present in a minority of cells) alteration. Clonal mutations are more reliable therapy targets because they represent the dominant tumor biology. [INTERPRETATION]

**MSI-H or TMB-H detected** represents a distinct category of actionable finding. These are not gene-specific mutations but system-level properties of the tumor's genomic landscape. MSI-H tumors have defective DNA mismatch repair (MMR), leading to accumulation of insertions and deletions at microsatellite regions across the genome. This generates a high neoantigen burden — the tumor produces many abnormal proteins that the immune system can recognize. [FACT] This is why MSI-H tumors respond to immune checkpoint inhibitors: the immune system already recognizes the tumor as foreign, it just needs the checkpoint brake released. TMB-H operates through a similar logic — more total mutations means more neoantigens, means more immune targets. The FDA granted pan-tumor approval to pembrolizumab for both MSI-H and TMB-H tumors based on this mechanism. [FACT]

**No ctDNA detected or no actionable mutations** requires careful interpretation. Not all tumors shed equivalent amounts of ctDNA. Brain tumors (protected by the blood-brain barrier), renal cell carcinomas, and certain indolent tumors may have very low ctDNA levels even at advanced stages. [INTERPRETATION] A negative Guardant360 in a patient with known advanced cancer does NOT mean the cancer lacks mutations — it may mean the mutations are present in the tissue but not sufficiently represented in the blood. In this scenario, tissue biopsy (FoundationOne CDx or equivalent) is the appropriate next step. The two platforms — blood and tissue — are complementary, not interchangeable. [FACT]

### Pattern Recognition

- **Guardant360 positive for targetable mutation + rising VAF on serial testing** = tumor is growing and the dominant clone carries the target. Initiate targeted therapy promptly. The VAF trend adds temporal context that a single measurement cannot provide. [INTERPRETATION]
- **Guardant360 shows new mutation emerging during targeted therapy** = resistance mechanism detected. Many targeted therapies eventually select for resistance mutations (e.g., EGFR T790M after first-generation EGFR inhibitors, EGFR C797S after osimertinib). Detecting these via liquid biopsy before clinical progression allows preemptive therapy changes. [FACT]
- **Guardant360 MSI-H detected + no prior Lynch syndrome screening** = check germline MMR gene status (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2). Up to 15–20% of MSI-H tumors in certain cancer types have underlying Lynch syndrome, which has implications for the patient's relatives. [FACT]
- **Guardant360 negative in a patient with progressive advanced cancer** = ctDNA shedding below detection threshold. Consider tissue biopsy, alternative liquid biopsy with higher sensitivity for low-shedding tumors, or empiric therapy based on tumor type and clinical features. Do not assume "no mutations" from a negative blood test. [INTERPRETATION]

### Intervention Levers

- **Targeted therapy matching:** The primary intervention lever. Each detected mutation maps to a specific therapeutic pathway. Maintain current knowledge of NCCN Compendium and FDA companion diagnostic approvals — the list evolves quarterly. [FACT]
- **Resistance monitoring via serial testing:** Repeat Guardant360 at 8–12 week intervals during targeted therapy to detect emerging resistance mutations before clinical progression is evident on imaging. This enables "interception" — switching therapies at molecular rather than radiographic progression. [INTERPRETATION]
- **Immunotherapy candidacy:** MSI-H or TMB-H detection opens checkpoint inhibitor pathway regardless of tumor type (pan-tumor indication). [FACT]
- **Clinical trial matching:** Many mutations detected by Guardant360 may not yet have approved therapies but are active targets in clinical trials (e.g., KRAS G12C inhibitors, HER2 mutations in non-breast cancers, FGFR alterations). The test result should trigger a clinical trial search alongside standard therapy evaluation. [INTERPRETATION]
- **Metabolic and immune support (adjunctive):** While not directly modifying the genomic landscape detected by Guardant360, the body's capacity to respond to targeted therapy and immunotherapy is modulated by nutritional status, inflammation, gut microbiome integrity, and immune function. Ensure adequate protein intake (1.2–1.5 g/kg), vitamin D optimization (40–60 ng/mL), omega-3 fatty acids for anti-inflammatory support, and probiotics or fermented foods to support gut-immune axis during immunotherapy. [INTERPRETATION]

### Body Codx Three-Density Reading

**Body:** Guardant360 reads the Defense operation at its most granular molecular level — the specific genomic alterations driving a tumor's behavior and vulnerability. At body density, this is pure signal intelligence: what mutations does the enemy carry, and which weapons in the therapeutic arsenal match those mutations? The 74-gene panel is a targeted interrogation of the tumor's molecular identity, performed through the blood rather than through surgical access to the tumor itself. The variant allele frequency adds a quantitative dimension — not just what mutations exist, but how dominant they are in the tumor population. This is the Defense operation gathering tactical intelligence for a targeted strike. [INTERPRETATION]

**Soul:** At soul density, Guardant360 represents Discernment — the capacity to identify the precise nature of the threat and match the correct response. Cancer therapy without genomic profiling is fighting blind — applying broad-spectrum chemotherapy because you cannot see what specific vulnerability the tumor carries. Genomic profiling is the soul operation of discernment applied to medicine: rather than responding to cancer with undifferentiated force, you identify the specific distortion and apply the specific correction. The soul question is not "how do we kill this?" but "what exactly is this, and what is its specific weakness?" That specificity — the willingness to see the precise nature of what has gone wrong rather than treating everything the same — is the essence of Discernment. [INTERPRETATION]

**Spirit:** At spirit density, the serial monitoring capacity of Guardant360 maps to Awareness as vigilance — the sustained attention that detects change before it becomes crisis. Resistance mutations emerge in the molecular shadow long before they appear as tumor growth on a scan. The capacity to detect this shift — to know that the ground has changed beneath you before the structure visibly cracks — is a form of molecular awareness. The spirit reading is about the relationship between the observer and the observed: the cancer is evolving, and the question is whether you are paying attention at the resolution that matters. [INTERPRETATION]

### Clinical Pearl

Guardant360's deepest clinical value is in the metastatic setting where tissue is scarce and time is precious. A patient with advanced NSCLC who has had a lung biopsy showing adenocarcinoma but insufficient tissue for molecular testing faces a dilemma: repeat biopsy (with associated risks and delay) or empiric chemotherapy (without knowing if a targetable mutation exists). Guardant360 resolves this in 7–14 days from a blood draw. The concordance between Guardant360 and tissue-based testing for common mutations is approximately 80–90%, with discordance often representing tumor heterogeneity (blood captures all shedding sites, tissue captures only the biopsied site) rather than test error. [NEEDS VERIFICATION] The clinical discipline is understanding the asymmetry: a positive Guardant360 finding can be acted upon immediately, but a negative finding in the setting of known cancer should prompt consideration of tissue testing rather than being accepted as definitively negative.

---

## FoundationOne Liquid CDx

**Body Codx Operation:** Defense
**What It Measures:** Comprehensive liquid biopsy analyzing circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) across 324 cancer-relevant genes for genomic alterations, microsatellite instability (MSI), tumor mutational burden (TMB), and blood-based TMB (bTMB) — FDA-approved companion diagnostic for multiple targeted therapies.
**Sample Type:** Blood (two 10 mL Streck cfDNA BCT tubes)
**Fasting Required:** No
**Test Developer:** Foundation Medicine (a Roche company)
**FDA Status:** FDA-approved companion diagnostic for multiple targeted therapy indications

### What This Number Means

FoundationOne Liquid CDx is the blood-based counterpart to FoundationOne CDx (tissue), expanding genomic profiling from 324 tissue genes to 324 genes analyzed from ctDNA in the bloodstream. [FACT] The test is specifically designed as an FDA-approved companion diagnostic — meaning the FDA has formally linked specific test results on this platform to specific therapy approvals. This is a critical distinction from laboratory-developed tests (LDTs) that may provide similar information but lack formal FDA companion diagnostic designation. When FoundationOne Liquid CDx reports an EGFR exon 19 deletion, that specific finding on that specific platform is the FDA-recognized basis for prescribing osimertinib. [FACT]

The 324-gene panel is substantially broader than Guardant360's 74-gene panel, covering not just the most commonly actionable genes but also genes with emerging therapeutic relevance, genes involved in resistance mechanisms, and genes with prognostic significance. [FACT] The test detects four categories of genomic alterations: base substitutions (point mutations), insertions and deletions (indels), copy number alterations (gene amplifications and homozygous deletions), and select gene rearrangements (fusions). It also reports two system-level biomarkers: MSI status (MSI-H vs. MSS via analysis of >100 microsatellite loci) and blood TMB (mutations per megabase of sequenced DNA). [FACT] The broader panel means more genes interrogated per test, but the clinical significance of findings in genes outside the most established targets requires careful interpretation — not every detected mutation is actionable.

### Ranges

| Marker | Conventional Reference | Functional Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Tier I alteration detected (FDA-approved therapy match) | Direct therapeutic indication | Act on this — companion diagnostic designation applies |
| Tier II alteration (strong clinical evidence) | Guideline-recommended therapy | Cross-reference NCCN guidelines for cancer type |
| Tier III alteration (emerging evidence) | Potential clinical trial match | Do not treat based on this alone — trial enrollment appropriate |
| Variant of unknown significance (VUS) | No current clinical actionability | Document and monitor — significance may be clarified by future research |
| MSI-H | Pembrolizumab (pan-tumor) | Check germline Lynch syndrome evaluation |
| MSS | Standard therapy | ~85% of solid tumors [FACT] |
| bTMB-H (≥16 mut/Mb) | Immune checkpoint inhibitor candidacy | Blood TMB and tissue TMB show moderate correlation (r ≈ 0.6–0.7) [NEEDS VERIFICATION] |
| bTMB-L | Standard therapy | Does not definitively exclude immunotherapy benefit |
| ctDNA fraction — high (>1%) | Substantial tumor shedding — reliable profiling | Higher confidence in detected and not-detected alterations |
| ctDNA fraction — low (<0.5%) | Low tumor shedding | Detected alterations are reliable; absent alterations may be false negatives |

### What Deviation Signals

**Tier I alteration detected with companion diagnostic designation** is the most clinically impactful finding. These are the specific gene-therapy pairs that the FDA has formally validated on this platform: EGFR mutations (osimertinib), ALK rearrangements (alectinib, crizotinib), BRCA1/2 mutations in prostate and ovarian cancer (olaparib, rucaparib), PIK3CA mutations in breast cancer (alpelisib), ERBB2 (HER2) amplification (trastuzumab-based regimens), BRAF V600E (encorafenib/binimetinib in colorectal), and several others. [FACT] The companion diagnostic designation means the FDA has reviewed analytical validity (does the test accurately detect the alteration?) and clinical validity (does detection of this alteration predict therapy response?) for each specific pairing. This carries regulatory weight that laboratory-developed tests do not.

**Broad genomic profiling with variants of unknown significance (VUS)** is a double-edged output. The 324-gene panel will inevitably detect variants in genes that are not yet clinically actionable — the VUS category. These require genomic literacy to interpret correctly. A VUS is not a "concerning finding" — it is a finding whose significance has not yet been established. Over time, some VUS will be reclassified as pathogenic (as more data accumulates) and others as benign. [FACT] The appropriate clinical response to a VUS is documentation and watchful waiting, not anxiety or empiric treatment. However, VUS in emerging therapeutic targets should trigger a clinical trial database search — the patient may be eligible for basket trials enrolling patients with specific molecular alterations regardless of cancer type.

**Blood TMB (bTMB) discordance with tissue TMB** is an important interpretive consideration. Blood-based TMB calculation analyzes only the mutations detectable in ctDNA, which may not represent the full mutational landscape of the tumor tissue. Studies show moderate correlation (r ≈ 0.6–0.7) between bTMB and tissue TMB, with blood-based calculation sometimes underestimating the true mutational burden due to the fraction of mutations not shed into the bloodstream. [NEEDS VERIFICATION] When bTMB is high, this is likely reliable (the blood captured enough mutations to cross the threshold). When bTMB is low, tissue testing may be warranted if immunotherapy candidacy is clinically important — the tissue may harbor more mutations than the blood reveals.

### Pattern Recognition

- **FoundationOne Liquid CDx positive for companion diagnostic alteration** = the strongest possible finding. FDA-validated gene-therapy match on an FDA-approved platform. Initiate targeted therapy with confidence in the molecular rationale. [FACT]
- **Multiple actionable alterations detected simultaneously** = complex clinical decision. May indicate tumor heterogeneity (different clones carrying different drivers) or co-occurring mutations that affect therapy response. Example: EGFR mutation + TP53 co-mutation is associated with worse outcomes on EGFR-targeted therapy compared to EGFR mutation alone. [INTERPRETATION]
- **FoundationOne Liquid CDx negative + FoundationOne CDx tissue positive** = the blood missed what the tissue caught. This occurs in ~10–20% of cases and represents the inherent limitation of ctDNA-based testing: not all tumors shed equally. [NEEDS VERIFICATION] Do not rely exclusively on liquid biopsy when tissue is available.
- **Rising ctDNA fraction on serial testing** = disease progression at the molecular level, potentially weeks to months before radiographic progression. This can serve as an early indicator for therapy adjustment. [INTERPRETATION]

### Intervention Levers

- **Companion diagnostic-guided therapy:** Follow FDA-approved companion diagnostic pairings as primary intervention pathway. These carry the strongest regulatory and evidentiary basis. [FACT]
- **Comprehensive genomic report integration:** Share the full report with a molecular tumor board — many institutions now have these — for expert interpretation of findings beyond the obvious Tier I alterations. [INTERPRETATION]
- **Clinical trial matching:** The 324-gene panel generates a rich molecular profile that can be matched against open clinical trials via databases like ClinicalTrials.gov, MatchMiner, or NCI-MATCH. Particularly valuable for Tier II and III alterations without approved therapies. [INTERPRETATION]
- **Serial liquid biopsy for resistance monitoring:** As with Guardant360, repeat testing during therapy tracks the molecular evolution of the tumor — emerging resistance mutations, shifting clonal architecture, or new actionable targets appearing under therapeutic pressure. [INTERPRETATION]
- **Complementary tissue testing:** When liquid biopsy is negative or inconclusive but clinical suspicion of actionable alterations remains high, pursue tissue-based FoundationOne CDx for the full 324-gene tissue profile. The two platforms together provide the most comprehensive molecular characterization available. [FACT]

### Body Codx Three-Density Reading

**Body:** At body density, FoundationOne Liquid CDx is the Defense operation's intelligence network operating at maximum resolution — 324 genes interrogated from a blood draw, FDA-companion diagnostic designations linking specific findings to specific therapies. This is molecular warfare at its most precise: identify the target, match the weapon, deploy with regulatory confidence. The 324-gene breadth means the test captures not just the obvious drivers but also the co-mutations, resistance mechanisms, and prognostic factors that modify the primary finding. The body is reading its own distress signals with the highest-resolution instrument currently available in blood-based genomic profiling. [INTERPRETATION]

**Soul:** At soul density, the companion diagnostic framework represents Meaning — the regulatory and evidentiary architecture that transforms a raw molecular finding into a therapeutic directive. An EGFR mutation detected on a non-FDA-approved platform and the same mutation detected on FoundationOne Liquid CDx carry different institutional weight: insurance coverage, treatment authorization, legal defensibility. This is the meaning-making apparatus of the medical system — how a biological signal becomes a treatment decision. The soul question is about the relationship between evidence and action: when is the evidence strong enough to act? The companion diagnostic framework is the medical system's answer to that question. [INTERPRETATION]

**Spirit:** At spirit density, the 324-gene panel represents the aspiration of comprehensive awareness — seeing the full molecular landscape rather than a narrow slice. Most cancers are not driven by a single mutation but by a complex interplay of alterations. The broader panel is an engineering commitment to seeing more of the whole, even when the clinical actionability of much of what is seen remains uncertain. The VUS category — variants of unknown significance — is itself a spiritual teaching: the willingness to see what you cannot yet understand, to hold data without premature closure, to maintain awareness of what is present even when its meaning is not yet clear. [INTERPRETATION]

### Clinical Pearl

The practical clinical difference between FoundationOne Liquid CDx and Guardant360 is less about which is "better" and more about which is FDA-validated for the specific therapeutic decision at hand. Both platforms are analytically excellent, but FDA companion diagnostic approvals are platform-specific. If the treatment decision hinges on an EGFR mutation and the oncologist needs to prescribe osimertinib with insurance authorization, the specific platform that carries the companion diagnostic approval for that drug matters. In practice, many oncologists order whichever platform their institution has a standing relationship with, and for most common actionable mutations, the concordance between platforms is high. The deeper clinical discipline is understanding that liquid biopsy is a complement to tissue biopsy, not a replacement — and that the ideal workup for any advanced cancer patient includes both whenever feasible.

---

## Signatera (Molecular Residual Disease)

**Body Codx Operation:** Restoration
**What It Measures:** Tumor-informed, personalized circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) assay tracking up to 16 patient-specific somatic variants for molecular residual disease (MRD) detection, recurrence monitoring, and treatment response assessment.
**Sample Type:** Blood (two 10 mL Streck cfDNA BCT tubes) + prior tumor tissue for assay design
**Fasting Required:** No
**Test Developer:** Natera, Inc.
**FDA Status:** FDA breakthrough device designation for multiple cancer types; Medicare coverage for colorectal cancer

### What This Number Means

Signatera occupies a fundamentally different niche than the screening and therapy-selection tools above. It is not looking for unknown cancers or unknown mutations. It is looking for *known* mutations — specifically, the exact mutations that were identified in the patient's own surgically resected tumor tissue — in the patient's blood after treatment. [FACT] This makes it a molecular residual disease (MRD) test: after surgery, chemotherapy, or radiation has treated the cancer, is there any detectable tumor DNA still circulating? If yes, cancer cells persist somewhere in the body even if imaging shows no evidence of disease. If no, the treatment may have been curative — or at least, any remaining disease is below the assay's detection threshold.

The process begins with whole-exome sequencing (WES) of the patient's tumor tissue and matched normal DNA (typically from white blood cells). A bioinformatics algorithm selects up to 16 clonal somatic mutations that are highly specific to the tumor and abundant in the tumor tissue — maximizing the probability that these variants would be detectable in blood if any tumor cells remain. [FACT] A custom PCR-based assay is then manufactured specifically for that patient, targeting those 16 variants simultaneously. Subsequent blood draws are analyzed with this personalized assay, achieving extraordinary analytical sensitivity — detection of ctDNA at levels as low as 0.01% (1 mutant molecule per 10,000 cfDNA molecules). [FACT] The result is reported as "ctDNA Positive" (MRD detected — residual cancer likely) or "ctDNA Negative" (no MRD detected — no evidence of residual disease at this sensitivity level).

### Ranges

| Marker | Conventional Reference | Functional Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| ctDNA Positive (MRD detected) | Residual cancer cells present | Recurrence risk markedly elevated — consider adjuvant therapy or intensified surveillance |
| ctDNA Negative (MRD not detected) | No residual disease detected at assay sensitivity | Favorable prognosis — but does not guarantee cure |
| ctDNA clearance (positive → negative during treatment) | Treatment response at molecular level | Correlates with improved outcomes [FACT] |
| ctDNA persistence (remains positive post-treatment) | Treatment has not eradicated MRD | High recurrence risk — consider additional or alternative therapy |
| ctDNA conversion (negative → positive on surveillance) | Molecular recurrence — precedes clinical recurrence | Lead time of 3–12 months before radiographic detection in many cancer types [NEEDS VERIFICATION] |
| Mean tumor molecules per mL (MTM/mL) | Quantitative ctDNA burden | Higher levels correlate with larger residual disease volume [INTERPRETATION] |
| Sensitivity (analytic) | ~0.01% ctDNA fraction detectable [FACT] | Among the most sensitive MRD assays available |
| Specificity | >99.8% per patient-specific assay [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | False positive rate extremely low due to tumor-informed design |

### What Deviation Signals

**ctDNA Positive post-surgery** is the most clinically significant finding. In colorectal cancer — where Signatera has the deepest evidence base — patients who are ctDNA-positive after curative-intent surgery have a dramatically higher recurrence rate compared to ctDNA-negative patients. Studies in Stage II and III CRC show that ctDNA-positive patients have a hazard ratio for recurrence of approximately 7–18x compared to ctDNA-negative patients, depending on the study and timepoint. [NEEDS VERIFICATION] This signal is now being used in clinical decision-making: ctDNA-positive patients may benefit from adjuvant chemotherapy even in stages where the standard recommendation is observation (e.g., Stage II CRC without high-risk features), while ctDNA-negative patients may safely de-escalate or omit adjuvant therapy. The randomized DYNAMIC trial demonstrated that ctDNA-guided therapy in Stage II CRC reduced chemotherapy use by nearly half without compromising recurrence-free survival. [NEEDS VERIFICATION]

**ctDNA clearance during treatment** — a shift from positive to negative — is a real-time molecular indicator of treatment response. When the chemotherapy or immunotherapy is working, the tumor cells die, stop shedding DNA, and the blood clears. This molecular response often precedes radiographic response (tumor shrinkage on imaging) by weeks to months, providing earlier confirmation that the treatment is effective. [INTERPRETATION] Conversely, ctDNA persistence through treatment — remaining positive despite therapy — is an ominous sign indicating the tumor is not responding and alternative strategies should be considered early rather than waiting for imaging to confirm progression.

**ctDNA conversion on surveillance** — a shift from negative to positive during post-treatment monitoring — is the molecular herald of recurrence. This is perhaps Signatera's most powerful application: detecting recurrence months before it becomes visible on CT, MRI, or PET scans. The lead time varies by cancer type and individual but has been reported at 3–12 months in multiple studies. [NEEDS VERIFICATION] This window is clinically actionable — earlier detection of recurrence, when the tumor burden is minimal, may translate to better outcomes from salvage therapy, surgical resection of oligometastatic disease, or enrollment in clinical trials with better eligibility profiles.

### Pattern Recognition

- **Post-surgical ctDNA negative → remains negative at 3, 6, 12 months** = most favorable molecular trajectory. Consistent with curative resection. Continue standard surveillance, but the molecular reassurance is substantial. [INTERPRETATION]
- **Post-surgical ctDNA positive → clears with adjuvant chemotherapy → remains negative** = treatment achieved molecular remission. The chemotherapy eliminated residual disease that surgery left behind. This is the adjuvant therapy success story at the molecular level. [INTERPRETATION]
- **Post-surgical ctDNA positive → persists through adjuvant chemotherapy** = treatment failure at the molecular level. Consider more aggressive second-line approaches, enrollment in clinical trials, or comprehensive genomic profiling (Guardant360 or FoundationOne Liquid CDx) to identify targetable mutations in the persistent disease. [INTERPRETATION]
- **ctDNA negative for 12+ months → converts to positive** = late molecular recurrence. Initiate comprehensive restaging imaging and consider repeat genomic profiling — the recurrent disease may carry new mutations not present in the original tumor. [INTERPRETATION]

### Intervention Levers

- **Adjuvant therapy escalation/de-escalation:** The primary clinical intervention. ctDNA-positive patients may benefit from adjuvant therapy even in lower-risk stages. ctDNA-negative patients may safely forego or abbreviate adjuvant therapy. This represents true precision medicine — treating the molecular reality, not just the statistical risk. [INTERPRETATION]
- **Surveillance intensification or relaxation:** ctDNA-positive patients warrant more frequent imaging and shorter follow-up intervals. ctDNA-negative patients may be safely followed with standard surveillance schedules, reducing anxiety and healthcare utilization. [INTERPRETATION]
- **Recurrence lead-time intervention:** When ctDNA converts from negative to positive, the lead time before clinical recurrence allows for (a) early imaging to localize disease, (b) consideration of curative-intent surgery for oligometastatic recurrence, and (c) initiation of systemic therapy at minimal disease burden rather than waiting for symptomatic progression. [INTERPRETATION]
- **Immune and metabolic optimization during surveillance:** The ctDNA-negative window is not passive waiting — it is an active opportunity to optimize the body's own anti-tumor capacity. Regular exercise (150+ min/week moderate intensity), metabolic health optimization (fasting insulin <5 µIU/mL, HbA1c <5.4%), vitamin D sufficiency (40–60 ng/mL), adequate sleep (7–9 hours), stress management, and anti-inflammatory nutrition all support the immune surveillance that must maintain remission. [INTERPRETATION]
- **Psychosocial support for molecular monitoring:** The emotional burden of serial ctDNA testing is real. Each blood draw represents a moment of vulnerability — the possibility that the cancer has returned. Patients need preparation for the psychological weight of longitudinal molecular monitoring, and support structures for managing the anxiety of repeated testing cycles. [INTERPRETATION]

### Body Codx Three-Density Reading

**Body:** At body density, Signatera is the Restoration operation's verification instrument — a molecular confirmation that repair has been successful. After surgery removes the visible tumor and chemotherapy destroys residual cells, Signatera asks the definitive question: did the Restoration succeed? The personalized assay design — custom-built from the patient's own tumor mutations — is an engineering solution to the signal-to-noise problem. By knowing exactly which 16 mutations to look for, the assay achieves extraordinary sensitivity (0.01% ctDNA fraction), far surpassing what a broad panel can achieve for any single target. This is the body asking its own specific question in its own specific language: are the exact cells that threatened me still present? [INTERPRETATION]

**Soul:** At soul density, Signatera maps to Restoration as the capacity to confirm that what was broken has been healed — and to detect when healing is incomplete. The soul question after any significant rupture (illness, loss, trauma) is: am I actually past it, or is something still active beneath the surface? Signatera gives this question a molecular answer. The ctDNA-negative result is not just a lab finding — it is existential reassurance that the threat has been addressed. The ctDNA-positive result is the opposite: the recognition that the work is not done, that something persists even when the surface looks clear. The soul reading is about the honesty of this assessment — the willingness to check whether healing is real rather than assumed. [INTERPRETATION]

**Spirit:** At spirit density, Signatera's longitudinal monitoring maps to the Return pathway — the capacity to learn from what has happened and remain vigilant without becoming consumed by fear. Post-cancer surveillance is a spiritual practice: the person must hold the tension between genuine recovery and the possibility of recurrence without letting either truth collapse the other. The ctDNA test at each interval is a moment of present-moment awareness — are you here, now, free? Or has something returned? The molecular lead time that Signatera provides — knowing before the body shows visible evidence — is a form of prescient awareness. The spirit question is: can you remain fully alive in the present while maintaining awareness of what may be gathering in the unseen? This is the fundamental tension of all post-crisis existence, and Signatera renders it in molecular terms. [INTERPRETATION]

### Clinical Pearl

Signatera's deepest clinical impact is in the de-escalation story. For decades, adjuvant chemotherapy decisions have been made on statistical risk: "You have a Stage III colon cancer, so you have a 30% chance of recurrence, so we recommend 6 months of chemotherapy." This exposes 70% of patients to toxic therapy they do not need. Signatera allows the question to shift from "what is your statistical risk?" to "do you have detectable residual disease right now?" The Stage III patient who is ctDNA-negative post-surgery may genuinely not need adjuvant therapy — and randomized evidence is accumulating to support this ctDNA-guided approach. This is not just a better test — it is a fundamentally different model of cancer treatment: treating the biology, not the statistics.

---

## CancerSEEK

**Body Codx Operation:** Cross-Operation (Defense / Transduction / Regulation)
**What It Measures:** Multi-analyte blood test combining circulating tumor DNA mutations in 16 cancer-driver genes with 8 protein biomarkers to detect early-stage cancer across 8 organ types (ovary, liver, stomach, pancreas, esophagus, colorectal, lung, breast).
**Sample Type:** Blood
**Fasting Required:** Not established (likely preferred for protein biomarker stability)
**Test Developer:** Thrive Earlier Detection (now Exact Sciences, following acquisition)
**Status:** Research/investigational — not yet FDA-approved for routine clinical use [FACT]

### What This Number Means

CancerSEEK represents a fundamentally different engineering approach to early cancer detection. Rather than relying solely on cfDNA (like Galleri) or solely on protein biomarkers (like CA-125 or CEA), it combines both modalities — somatic mutations in ctDNA and circulating protein levels — into a single composite score. [FACT] The logic: mutations and proteins provide orthogonal (non-redundant) information. A tumor may shed detectable ctDNA but not yet produce elevated protein levels, or vice versa. By combining both signals, the test achieves higher sensitivity than either alone while maintaining specificity through a machine learning algorithm that integrates the two data streams.

The ctDNA component analyzes 2,001 genomic positions across 16 genes that are frequently mutated in cancer — including TP53, KRAS, PIK3CA, APC, PTEN, CTNNB1, and others that are recurrently altered across the 8 target cancer types. [FACT] The protein component measures 8 circulating proteins: CA-125 (ovarian), CA 19-9 (pancreatic/GI), CEA (colorectal/general), HGF (hepatocyte growth factor), OPN (osteopontin), PRL (prolactin), TIMP-1 (tissue inhibitor of metalloproteinases-1), and myeloperoxidase. [NEEDS VERIFICATION] The machine learning algorithm weighs the mutation and protein inputs together with clinical variables (age, sex) to produce a probability score for cancer presence, along with a tissue-of-origin prediction identifying which of the 8 organ types is the likely source.

### Ranges

| Marker | Conventional Reference | Functional Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| CancerSEEK score — negative | No cancer signal detected | Reassuring but sensitivity limited in Stage I disease |
| CancerSEEK score — positive | Cancer signal detected — requires confirmatory workup | Tissue-of-origin prediction directs diagnostic pathway |
| Median sensitivity — overall (Stage I–III) | ~62% across 8 cancer types [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | Substantially variable by cancer type and stage |
| Sensitivity — Stage I | ~40% across cancer types [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | Lowest for breast cancer, highest for liver and ovarian |
| Sensitivity — Stage III | ~78% across cancer types [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | Higher tumor burden improves detection |
| Specificity | >99% in initial validation cohort [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | Critical for any screening application — false positives must be rare |
| Sensitivity by cancer type — liver | ~90%+ [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | Highest among the 8 target organs |
| Sensitivity by cancer type — ovarian | ~98% Stage I-III [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | Exceptionally high — no current standard screening exists |
| Sensitivity by cancer type — breast | ~33% [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | Lowest — mammography remains superior for breast cancer |
| Tissue-of-origin accuracy | ~63% on top-1 prediction, ~83% on top-2 [NEEDS VERIFICATION] | Less precise than Galleri's methylation-based TOO |

### What Deviation Signals

**CancerSEEK positive with high confidence score and concordant tissue-of-origin** suggests an early-stage cancer in one of the 8 target organ types. The clinical pathway mirrors Galleri: confirm the tissue-of-origin prediction with directed imaging, obtain tissue diagnosis through biopsy, and proceed with standard staging and treatment. The strength of CancerSEEK is its sensitivity for specific cancers that lack standard screening — particularly ovarian, pancreatic, liver, esophageal, and gastric cancers, all of which are typically diagnosed at advanced stages. [INTERPRETATION] The multi-analyte approach may capture some tumors that a methylation-only approach misses, and vice versa — the two modalities are complementary in their biology.

**CancerSEEK positive with low confidence score or ambiguous tissue-of-origin** requires more cautious interpretation. The protein biomarkers included in the panel (CA-125, CEA, CA 19-9) can be elevated by non-malignant conditions — endometriosis or ovarian cysts (CA-125), inflammatory bowel disease or smoking (CEA), pancreatitis or biliary obstruction (CA 19-9). [FACT] When the protein signal is driving the positive score but the ctDNA component is negative, the pre-test probability of cancer is lower than when both components are positive. In this scenario, a stepwise diagnostic approach with repeat testing in 3–6 months may be more appropriate than an immediate invasive workup, depending on clinical context and patient risk factors.

**CancerSEEK negative in a high-risk individual** has the same limitation as any screening test with imperfect sensitivity: a negative result reduces but does not eliminate the probability of cancer. The test's sensitivity for Stage I disease (~40% overall) means that most early-stage cancers will be missed on any single test. [NEEDS VERIFICATION] Serial testing over time increases the cumulative probability of detection, but no single negative result can provide absolute reassurance. Clinical judgment and standard screening protocols remain paramount.

### Pattern Recognition

- **CancerSEEK positive with both ctDNA mutations AND elevated protein biomarkers** = highest confidence finding. Both orthogonal signals agree. Proceed urgently with directed workup based on tissue-of-origin prediction. [INTERPRETATION]
- **CancerSEEK positive with ctDNA mutations only (proteins normal)** = early molecular signal, possibly very early stage where the tumor has begun shedding DNA but has not yet produced measurable systemic protein effects. Still requires workup, but the tumor burden may be minimal. [HYPOTHESIS]
- **CancerSEEK positive with elevated proteins only (no ctDNA mutations)** = potential false positive from non-malignant protein elevation, or a cancer with low ctDNA shedding. Interpret cautiously.
